mappings.rationaleAI: Muhammad's life, career, revelations, Hijra, and establishment of Islam directly address the life and career of Muhammad and the rise of Islam (B.1.b).
Ali as fourth Rashidun caliph, the civil conflicts and origins of Shi'ism directly address Uthman and Ali's caliphates, social and religious grievances, and origins of Shi'ism (B.1.c.ii).
Shia Islam's origins in the succession dispute and the Karbala tragedy directly relate to the origins of Shi'ism under the early caliphs (B.1.c.ii).
The Quran as revelation compiled under Caliph Uthman connects to the rise of Islam (B.1.b) and the spread of Arabic language and literary revival under the Umayyads (B.2.d.ii).
The Islamic Golden Age's intellectual production under the Abbasid caliphate directly addresses cultural life under the Abbasid caliphate (C.1.d).
Avicenna's philosophical and medical works at the height of the Abbasid era directly address religion, theology, and philosophy (C.1.d.i) and the arts and sciences (C.1.d.ii) under the Abbasids.
Spain's Muslim Al-Andalus and Reconquista directly connect to the Umayyad emirate and caliphate in Spain (C.2.a).
Egypt's Islamic conquest in 641 CE connects to Muslim expansion outside Arabia (B.1.c.i). The Fatimid conquest of Egypt relates to the Fatimid state (C.2.b).
Iran's successive Islamic dynasties including the Buyid dynasty connect to the Buyid dynasty in Iran and Iraq (C.2.e).
Algeria's successive Islamic dynasties connect to other dynasties in North Africa including the Rustamid kingdom in the central Maghrib (C.2.c).
Arabic as the language of Islamic civilization and its spread as lingua franca directly address the spread of the Arabic language and literary revival under the Umayyads (B.2.d.ii).
The Byzantine Empire's loss of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to Arab conquests connects to Muslim expansion under the early caliphs (B.1.c.i).
Saudi Arabia's pre-Islamic Arabian history connects to Arabia before Muhammad (B.1.a).
Silk Road trade facilitation during the Abbasid era connects to economic and social life including manufactures and trade under the Abbasids (C.1.c).
Charlemagne's defeat of Muslim forces at Poitiers by Charles Martel (732) is referenced in the context of Muslim expansion into Spain and France (B.2.c — referenced in C.2.a.i).
The post-classical period's Islamic expansion and Abbasid Golden Age broadly address both the rise of Islam (B) and the Abbasid Empire and successor states (C).
